_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Anne Arundel County, MD. Record survival, courthouse history, and the exceptional advantage of Annapolis as both county seat and state capital — Maryland State Archives in Annapolis holds one of the finest collections of colonial and state records in the nation.
display_name: Anne Arundel County, Maryland — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: Annapolis, MD — 8 Church Circle (Circuit Court); Maryland State Archives at 350 Rowe Boulevard
records_housed: modern court records at Circuit Court
historical records at Maryland State Archives

previous_courthouses.location: Various locations in the Providence/Annapolis area. County courts met at taverns and private residences before a dedicated courthouse was built.
previous_courthouses.fate: No permanent courthouse structure survived from this period. Records were kept by the clerk and stored in private buildings.

previous_courthouses.location: Annapolis — Church Circle area. The county courthouse shared the government quarter of Annapolis with the State House.
previous_courthouses.fate: The colonial-era courthouse was replaced by successive structures. Records survived in reasonably good condition thanks to Annapolis's role as the capital — government record-keeping infrastructure was better here than in most county seats.

previous_courthouses.location: Annapolis — Church Circle. Multiple renovations and expansions of the courthouse complex.
previous_courthouses.fate: Served continuously. Historical records progressively transferred to the Maryland State Archives beginning in the twentieth century.

record_losses.cause: Fire at the State House — some colonial records destroyed
records_survived: most county-level deeds and court records survived because they were kept separately from provincial records
land patents survive in provincial patent books
record_losses.description: A fire at the State House in 1704 destroyed some provincial records but did not significantly affect Anne Arundel County's own court and land records, which were maintained by the county clerk at a separate location. The loss is more significant for provincial-level research than for county-level genealogy.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The 1704 fire at the State House may have destroyed provincial records that document {ancestor_name}'s earliest interactions with the colonial government — but the county's own deed books and court records, kept separately, likely survived.
record_losses.source: Papenfuse, Edward C. et al. Maryland: A New Guide to the Old Line State. Baltimore: Johns Hopkins University Press, 1976.

deeds.completeness: Excellent. Anne Arundel County deed records survive from 1653 and are among the most complete colonial land record series in America. The Maryland State Archives holds the originals and has digitized extensively.
deeds.gaps: Minor gaps in the earliest decades. Post-1700 records are essentially complete.
deeds.repository: Maryland State Archives, Annapolis; Anne Arundel County Circuit Court
deeds.online_access: Maryland State Archives online (mdlandrec.net for land records); FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com MD collections

wills_estates.completeness: Very good. Will books and administration accounts survive from the mid-seventeenth century. Maryland's prerogative court system centralized probate at the provincial level, so many wills and inventories are at the Maryland State Archives regardless of county.
wills_estates.gaps: Some early loose papers lost. The bound volumes are substantially complete.
wills_estates.repository: Maryland State Archives (prerogative court records); Anne Arundel County Register of Wills
wills_estates.online_access: Maryland State Archives online; FamilySearch; Ancestry.com

marriages.completeness: Marriage licenses from 1777. Earlier marriages reconstructed from church records (St. Anne's Parish, All Hallows Parish) and court records.
marriages.gaps: No marriage licenses before 1777. Church records partially fill this gap — the Anglican parishes maintained registers from the early colonial period.
marriages.repository: Maryland State Archives; Anne Arundel County Clerk of Court
marriages.online_access: Maryland State Archives; FamilySearch; Ancestry.com MD marriages

court_minutes.completeness: Very good. County court proceedings survive from 1658 — among the oldest continuous county court records in English America.
court_minutes.gaps: Some volumes from the 1660s-1670s are fragmentary.
court_minutes.online_access: Maryland State Archives; some FamilySearch digitization

vital_records.completeness: Maryland statewide registration began 1898 but compliance was incomplete until the 1910s. Earlier vital events tracked through church records and county court entries.
vital_records.repository: Maryland Department of Health, Division of Vital Records; Maryland State Archives for delayed and historical certificates

church_records.parish: St. Anne's Parish (Church of England / Episcopal) — Annapolis; All Hallows Parish — South River
church_records.completeness: St. Anne's Parish register survives from the 1690s — baptisms, marriages, burials. All Hallows Parish vestry records from the same period. These are among the most important Anglican colonial records in Maryland.
church_records.repository: Maryland State Archives; Maryland Diocesan Archives; individual parish custody
church_records.online_access: Published transcriptions; Maryland State Archives; some FamilySearch

substitute_sources.source_type: Maryland State Archives — comprehensive colonial and state records
substitute_sources.description: The Maryland State Archives in Annapolis is one of the finest state archives in the nation. Its holdings include colonial land patents (from 1633), prerogative court records (wills and inventories), provincial court records, and the complete run of county records deposited by the circuit courts. For Anne Arundel County ancestors, the State Archives is the single most important repository.
substitute_sources.repository: Maryland State Archives, 350 Rowe Boulevard, Annapolis, MD
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Exceptional. The Archives' online finding aids and digitization programs make Maryland one of the most accessible states for genealogical research. The colonial land patent series documents the arrival of specific individuals through headright claims.
substitute_sources.source: Maryland State Archives. Guide to Government Records. Annapolis, 2005.

substitute_sources.source_type: Maryland Prerogative Court records — wills, inventories, administration accounts
substitute_sources.description: Maryland's colonial probate system was centralized at the provincial level through the Prerogative Court, which operated from 1635 to 1777. Wills, inventories, and administration accounts for all Maryland counties were filed with the Prerogative Court. These records are held at the Maryland State Archives and provide extraordinary detail — inventories list every item in a household, from livestock to spoons, with appraised values.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The inventories are among the most detailed in colonial America. A 1720 inventory from Anne Arundel County might list every enslaved person by name, every piece of furniture, every tool, and every book in the household.
substitute_sources.source: Carr, Lois Green, and Lorena S. Walsh. 'Inventories and the Analysis of Wealth and Consumption Patterns in St. Mary's County, Maryland, 1658–1777.' Historical Methods 13, no. 2 (1980).

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. Anne Arundel County was enumerated from the first federal census. Slave schedules (1850–1860) identify slaveholders but not enslaved individuals by name.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential baseline. The 1790 census for Anne Arundel County is among the earliest Virginia/Maryland returns.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Anne Arundel County, MD.

narrative_hooks: The Maryland State Archives in Annapolis holds {ancestor_name}'s records in one of the finest colonial record collections in America — if the paper trail exists anywhere, it exists there.
Anne Arundel County's deed books stretch back to 1653 — if {ancestor_name} bought, sold, or inherited land in the county, the record likely survives in Annapolis.

The Prerogative Court inventories are genealogical gold — if {ancestor_name} died in colonial Maryland, the inventory might list every item in the household, every enslaved person by name, and every debt owed or owed to.
St. Anne's Parish in Annapolis kept registers from the 1690s — if {ancestor_name} was baptized, married, or buried in the colonial capital, the church record may survive alongside the county record.
source_references: Maryland State Archives. Guide to Government Records. Annapolis, 2005.
Papenfuse, Edward C. et al. Maryland: A New Guide to the Old Line State. Baltimore: Johns Hopkins University Press, 1976.
Carr, Lois Green, and Lorena S. Walsh. 'Inventories and the Analysis of Wealth and Consumption Patterns in St. Mary's County, Maryland, 1658-1777.' Historical Methods 13, no. 2 (1980).
Coldham, Peter Wilson. Settlers of Maryland, 1679-1700. Baltimore: Genealogical Publishing Co., 1995.
